seasonalPresence: Year-round. Rut in October. Best viewing autumn.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. Martindale and the eastern fells.
whereToSee: Martindale is the place for red deer. The valley is a stronghold. Also Grizedale Forest, the Ullswater area, and the eastern fells. Rut in October is spectacular.
ukPopulation: Around 360,000 in the UK. Largest land mammal in Britain.
lakesPopulation: Martindale holds one of the most accessible populations in England. Grizedale and Ullswater too. You'll hear the rut before you see it.
description: Red deer are the monarchs of the Lakes. Martindale is the place. Dawn or dusk in October for the rut. You'll hear the stags roaring. Keep your distance. They're wild. The valley is a designated refuge.
identification: Large. Red-brown in summer, grey in winter. Stags have antlers. Hinds smaller. Unmistakeable.
tips: Martindale. October for the rut. Dawn or dusk. Take binoculars. Keep your distance. Join a guided walk if you want to get closer.

faq.question: When is the red deer rut in the Lake District?
faq.answer: October. The rut peaks in mid to late October. Martindale is the place. Dawn or dusk. You'll hear the stags roaring before you see them.

faq.question: Where is the best place to see red deer in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Martindale valley is the stronghold. Grizedale Forest and the Ullswater area also hold populations. Martindale is a designated refuge. Keep your distance.

faq.question: Can I join a guided red deer rut walk in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Yes. Several operators run guided walks in October. RSPB Haweswater and local guides offer rut watches. Book ahead. Dawn or dusk slots.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most visible at dawn and dusk.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. Or after rain when they're more active.
whereToSee: Rivers and lakes throughout the Lakes. The Derwent, Rothay, and the shores of Windermere and Ullswater. Look for tracks and spraints.
ukPopulation: Around 10,000 in England. Recovered from near extinction.
lakesPopulation: Present on most Lakeland rivers and lakes. Elusive. Dawn and dusk. Look for tracks on muddy banks.
description: Otters are back in the Lakes. They're elusive. Dawn and dusk. The Derwent, Rothay, Windermere shores. Look for tracks and spraints. You might get lucky. Patience pays.
identification: Long, sinuous. Brown fur. Flat tail. Webbed feet. Tracks show five toes.
tips: Dawn and dusk. Quiet stretches of river. Look for tracks on muddy banks. Spraints smell of fish and jasmine. Don't expect to see one. Be pleased if you do.

faq.question: Where can I see otters in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Rivers and lakes throughout. The Derwent, Rothay, and shores of Windermere and Ullswater. Dawn and dusk. Look for tracks and spraints on muddy banks.

faq.question: What time of day is best for otter watching in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Dawn and dusk. Or after rain when they're more active. They're elusive. Patience pays. Don't expect to see one. Be pleased if you do.

faq.question: How do I identify otter signs in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Tracks show five toes. Spraints smell of fish and jasmine. Look on muddy banks and rocks by rivers. Quiet stretches. The Derwent and Rothay are good.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most active in morning and late afternoon.
bestTimeOfDay: Morning. First few hours after dawn.
whereToSee: Grizedale Forest is the stronghold. Also Whinlatter, the Borrowdale valley, and conifer woodland throughout the Lakes. Feeders at visitor centres help.
ukPopulation: Around 140,000 in the UK. Declining. Grey squirrel competition and disease.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale holds one of England's best populations. Whinlatter too. The Lake District is a stronghold. Feeders at the visitor centres.
description: Red squirrels are the Lakes. Grizedale is the place. The visitor centre has feeders. Whinlatter too. Go in the morning. They're used to people. That ear tufts. No grey squirrels here. They've been kept out.
identification: Red-brown fur. Ear tufts. Bushy tail. No grey. Smaller than grey squirrel.
tips: Grizedale visitor centre. Morning. The feeders. Whinlatter has a red squirrel trail. Conifer woodland. They're easier to see than you think.

faq.question: Where is the best place to see red squirrels in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Grizedale Forest is the stronghold. The visitor centre has feeders. Whinlatter has a red squirrel trail. Go in the morning. They're used to people.

faq.question: What time of day are red squirrels most active in the Lake District?
faq.answer: Morning. First few hours after dawn. Late afternoon too. Grizedale and Whinlatter visitor centres have feeders. They're easier to see than you think.

faq.question: Are there grey squirrels in the Lake District?
faq.answer: The Lake District is a red squirrel stronghold. Grey squirrels have been kept out of the main conifer areas. Grizedale and Whinlatter are grey-free.

bestTimeOfDay: Dusk. Or use a trail camera.
whereToSee: Grizedale Forest, Whinlatter, the Borrowdale valley. Reintroduced to the Lakes. Elusive. Trail cameras at some sites.
ukPopulation: Around 3,700 in Britain. Mostly Scotland. Reintroduced to England.
lakesPopulation: Reintroduced to Grizedale and Whinlatter. Present but elusive. Trail cameras at the visitor centres sometimes catch them.
description: Pine martens are back in the Lakes. Reintroduced. Grizedale, Whinlatter. You won't see one. They're nocturnal. Trail cameras at the visitor centres. Check the screens. That's your best chance.
identification: Cat-sized. Brown fur. Cream throat patch. Long bushy tail. Weasel family.
tips: Trail cameras at Grizedale and Whinlatter visitor centres. Dusk if you're lucky. Don't expect to see one. They're elusive.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most active at dusk and dawn.
whereToSee: Woodland edge throughout the Lakes. Setts in banks. Don't disturb. Watch from a distance.
lakesPopulation: Common in Lakeland woodland. Setts in banks. Dusk is the time. Keep your distance. Don't use lights.
description: Badgers are everywhere in the Lakes. Woodland edge. Setts in banks. Dusk. They're shy. Don't disturb the sett. Watch from a distance. No lights. Let your eyes adjust.
identification: Grey, black and white striped face. Stocky. Low to the ground. Unmistakeable.
tips: Dusk. Woodland edge. Find a sett first in daylight. Don't approach. Watch from distance. No lights.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. White in winter, brown in summer.
bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. Or in winter when snow makes them visible.
whereToSee: High fells. The Helvellyn range, the Coniston fells, the eastern fells. Above the treeline. Introduced to the Lakes.
ukPopulation: Around 350,000 in Scotland. Small introduced populations in England.
lakesPopulation: Introduced to the Lake District fells. Helvellyn, Coniston Old Man. White in winter. Brown in summer. High ground.
description: Mountain hares live on the high fells. Helvellyn, Coniston. White in winter. Brown in summer. They're shy. You need to walk. Dawn and dusk. Or winter when the snow makes them stand out.
identification: Smaller than brown hare. White in winter. Brown in summer. Shorter ears than brown hare.
tips: High fells. Winter for white coat. Dawn and dusk. They're shy. Walk quietly.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Most active spring and summer.
whereToSee: Rivers and streams. The Derwent, Rothay, quiet stretches. That plop. Declining. Otters and mink have hit them.
lakesPopulation: The Derwent. Rothay. Quiet stretches. That plop. Declining. Look for burrows in banks. Latrines. Don't expect to see one.
description: Water voles plop. That sound. The Derwent. Rothay. Quiet stretches. Declining. Otters and mink. Look for burrows. Latrines. Round droppings. Don't expect to see one. Be pleased if you do.
identification: Round. Brown. Blunt nose. Small ears. Plop. Rat has pointed nose. Vole is rounder.
tips: Quiet river stretches. Look for burrows. Latrines. That plop. Declining. Don't expect to see one.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. White in winter in the north.
whereToSee: Moorland, woodland edge, drystone walls. The fells, Grizedale, Martindale. That bounding. Black tip on tail.
lakesPopulation: The fells. Moorland. Drystone walls. That bounding run. Black tip on tail. Weasel is smaller, no black tip. Common. Fast.
description: Stoats bound. That run. Black tip on tail. Weasel is smaller, no black tip. The fells. Moorland. Drystone walls. Common. Fast. You'll get a glimpse.
identification: Long. Brown. Black tip on tail. Bounding run. Larger than weasel. Weasel has no black tip.
tips: Moorland. Drystone walls. That bounding run. Black tip on tail. Fast glimpse.

whereToSee: Moorland, woodland, drystone walls. The fells, Grizedale. Smaller than stoat. No black tip. That dart.
lakesPopulation: The fells. Moorland. Smaller than stoat. No black tip on tail. That dart. Common. Hard to see. Fast.
description: Weasels are small. No black tip on tail. Stoat has black tip. That dart. The fells. Moorland. Common. Hard to see. Fast. You'll get a glimpse.
identification: Small. Brown. No black tip on tail. Smaller than stoat. That dart.
tips: Moorland. That dart. No black tip. Smaller than stoat. Fast glimpse.

bestTimeOfDay: Dawn and dusk. That mad March hare.
whereToSee: Farmland, grassland. The valleys, Martindale. That boxing in March. Long ears. Brown. Larger than rabbit.
lakesPopulation: Martindale. Farmland. Grassland. That boxing in March. Mad March hare. Long ears. Brown. Larger than rabbit. Dawn and dusk.
description: Brown hares box in March. That mad March hare. Martindale. Farmland. Long ears. Brown. Larger than rabbit. Dawn and dusk. Declining.
identification: Large. Brown. Long ears. Black tips. Larger than rabbit. No burrow. Above ground.
tips: March for boxing. Dawn and dusk. Martindale. Farmland. Long ears. Larger than rabbit.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Rut in July and August.
whereToSee: Woodland edge. Grizedale, Borrowdale, the valleys. Smaller than red deer. That white rump. Common.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Borrowdale. Woodland edge. Smaller than red deer. That white rump. Common. Dawn and dusk. You'll see them from the road.
description: Roe deer are smaller than red deer. That white rump. Grizedale. Borrowdale. Woodland edge. Common. Dawn and dusk. Rut in July and August. Different to red deer. You'll see them.
identification: Small. Brown. White rump. Buck has small antlers. No neck mane. Smaller than red deer.
tips: Woodland edge. Dawn and dusk. Grizedale. Borrowdale. That white rump. Common.

whereToSee: Everywhere. Villages, farmland, woodland edge. That brush. Red. Common. Dawn and dusk.
lakesPopulation: Everywhere. Villages. Farmland. Woodland edge. That brush. Red. Dawn and dusk. You'll see them. Common.
description: Foxes are everywhere. That brush. Red. Villages. Farmland. Dawn and dusk. You'll see them. Common. Don't feed them.
identification: Red. Bushy tail. White tip. Pointed ears. Unmistakeable.
tips: Dawn and dusk. Villages. Farmland. That brush. Common.

seasonalPresence: April to October. Hibernates in winter.
whereToSee: Villages, woodland edge, over water. The valleys. That flutter at dusk. Our commonest bat. Pipistrelle. Actually two species. Common and soprano.
lakesPopulation: Villages. Woodland edge. Over water. Dusk. That flutter. Our commonest bat. Common and soprano pipistrelle. Both occur. You need a bat detector to tell them apart.
description: Pipistrelles flutter at dusk. Our commonest bat. Villages. Woodland edge. Over water. Actually two species. Common and soprano. You need a bat detector. Dusk. April to October.
identification: Small. Flutters. Dusk. Bat detector for species. 45 kHz or 55 kHz.
tips: Dusk. Villages. Over water. That flutter. Bat detector helps.

whereToSee: Over lakes and rivers. Windermere, Ullswater, the Derwent. That skim. Low over water. Trails feet. Water bat.
lakesPopulation: Windermere. Ullswater. The Derwent. That skim. Low over water. Trails feet. Water bat. Dusk. Common. Easy to see over lakes.
description: Daubenton's bats skim. Low over water. Trails feet. Windermere. Ullswater. The Derwent. Water bat. Dusk. Common. Easy to see over lakes.
identification: Skims. Low over water. Trails feet. Brown. Bat detector 45-50 kHz.
tips: Lakes. Rivers. Dusk. That skim. Low over water. Easy to see.

whereToSee: Woodland. Grizedale, Borrowdale, Whinlatter. Those long ears. Flutters. Slower than pipistrelle. Gleaning. Picks from leaves.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Borrowdale. Woodland. Those long ears. Slower flight. Gleaning. Picks insects from leaves. Dusk. Common. Bat detector 25-50 kHz.
diet: Insects. Gleans from leaves. Also catches in flight.
description: Brown long-eared bats have those ears. Long. Grizedale. Borrowdale. Woodland. Slower flight. Gleaning. Picks from leaves. Dusk. Common.
identification: Long ears. Slow flight. Gleaning. Brown. Bat detector 25-50 kHz.
tips: Woodland. Dusk. Those ears. Slower than pipistrelle. Grizedale. Borrowdale.

whereToSee: Woodland, scrub. Grizedale, Borrowdale, Foulshaw Moss. That rustle. Small. Brown. Redder than field vole. Common.
lakesPopulation: Grizedale. Borrowdale. Woodland. Scrub. That rustle. Small. Brown. Redder than field vole. Common. You'll hear them. Rarely see them.
description: Bank voles rustle. Small. Brown. Redder than field vole. Grizedale. Borrowdale. Woodland. Scrub. Common. You'll hear them. Rarely see them. Shy.
identification: Small. Brown. Redder than field vole. Rounded face. Woodland.
tips: Woodland. That rustle. Redder than field vole. Common. Rarely see them.

whereToSee: Woodland, grassland, moorland. Everywhere. That rustle. High-pitched squeak. Small. Pointed nose. Common.
lakesPopulation: Everywhere. Woodland. Grassland. Moorland. That rustle. High-pitched squeak. Small. Pointed nose. Common. You'll hear them. Dead ones on paths. Cats kill them. They smell.
diet: Insects, worms. Eats constantly. High metabolism.
description: Common shrews squeak. High-pitched. Small. Pointed nose. Everywhere. Woodland. Grassland. They eat constantly. High metabolism. Dead ones on paths. Cats kill them. They smell. Don't pick them up. They bite.
identification: Small. Pointed nose. Brown. High-pitched squeak. Three-toned. Not a rodent.
tips: Everywhere. That rustle. High-pitched squeak. Dead ones on paths. Don't pick them up.

whereToSee: Ponds, tarns, wet ground. Throughout the Lakes. That spawn in spring. Clumps. Common.
lakesPopulation: Ponds. Tarns. Wet ground. Spawn in spring. February to April. Common. That clump of spawn. Don't move spawn. Disease.
description: Common frogs spawn in spring. That clump. February to April. Ponds. Tarns. Throughout the Lakes. Common. Don't move spawn. Disease. Ranavirus. Chytrid.
identification: Smooth. Brown. Dark patch behind eye. Spawn in clumps. Toad has string spawn.
tips: Spring. Ponds. That spawn. Clumps. Don't move it. Disease.

seasonalPresence: Year-round. Migration to ponds February to April.
bestTimeOfDay: Dusk. Or after rain. Migration nights.
whereToSee: Ponds, migration routes. Throughout the Lakes. That string spawn. Warty. Walks. Doesn't hop like frog.
lakesPopulation: Ponds. Migration routes. That string spawn. February to April. Warty. Walks. Doesn't hop. Common. Toad patrols help them cross roads.
description: Common toads are warty. They walk. Don't hop like frogs. String spawn. Not clumps. February to April. Migration to ponds. Toad patrols help them cross roads. Declining.
identification: Warty. Brown. Walks. String spawn. Frog has clumps. Toad has string.
tips: Spring. Migration. That string spawn. Warty. Walks. Toad patrols.

seasonalPresence: March to September in water. Terrestrial rest of year.
whereToSee: Ponds, tarns. Acid water. The fells, Grizedale. That filament on tail. Male. Webbed feet. Smaller than smooth newt.
lakesPopulation: Ponds. Tarns. Acid water. The fells. Grizedale. That filament on tail. Male. Webbed feet. Smaller than smooth newt. Common in upland ponds.
description: Palmate newts like acid water. The fells. Grizedale. That filament on tail. Male. Webbed feet. Smaller than smooth newt. Common in upland ponds. Torch at night. Net with care.
identification: Small. Filament on tail. Male. Webbed feet. Acid water. Smaller than smooth newt.
tips: Acid ponds. The fells. Torch at night. That filament. Male. Webbed feet.

seasonalPresence: March to October. Hibernates in winter.
whereToSee: Heath, moorland. Foulshaw Moss, Grizedale, the fells. That zigzag. Only venomous snake in Britain. Don't touch.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Heath. Moorland. That zigzag. Basking in morning. Only venomous snake in Britain. Don't touch. They're shy. They'll move. Give them space.
description: Adders have that zigzag. Only venomous snake in Britain. Foulshaw Moss. Heath. Moorland. Basking in morning. Don't touch. They're shy. They'll move. Give them space. Declining.
identification: Zigzag. Red eye. Vertical pupil. Only venomous snake. Don't touch.
tips: Heath. Moorland. Morning for basking. Don't touch. Give them space. They're shy.

whereToSee: Heath, moorland, drystone walls. Foulshaw Moss, Grizedale, the fells. That dart. Brown. Small. Only native lizard. Live-bearing.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Heath. Moorland. Drystone walls. That dart. Brown. Small. Only native lizard. Live-bearing. Basking in morning. Common.

whereToSee: Heath, grassland, gardens. Foulshaw Moss, Grizedale. That legless lizard. Not a snake. Blinks. Smooth. Common.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Heath. Grassland. That legless lizard. Not a snake. Blinks. Smooth. Common. Under refugia. Corrugated iron. They like warmth.
description: Slow worms are legless lizards. Not snakes. They blink. Snakes don't. Foulshaw Moss. Heath. Grassland. Under refugia. Corrugated iron. They like warmth. Common. Don't pick them up. Tail drops off.

whereToSee: Tall grassland, reedbeds. Foulshaw Moss, the valleys. That nest. Tennis ball. In grass. Our smallest rodent. Rare.
lakesPopulation: Foulshaw Moss. Tall grassland. Reedbeds. That nest. Tennis ball. In grass. Our smallest rodent. Rare. Don't expect to see one. Nests in winter. Look for them in cut grass.

whereToSee: Woodland, farmland. The eastern Lakes. Recovering. Rare. Often confused with ferret. Bandit mask. Dark. Road casualties. Don't expect to see one.
lakesPopulation: Eastern Lakes. Woodland. Farmland. Recovering. Rare. Bandit mask. Dark. Often confused with feral ferret. Polecat has dark mask, ferret has pale. Road casualties. Don't expect to see one.
description: Polecats are recovering. Bandit mask. Dark. Eastern Lakes. Rare. Often confused with feral ferret. Polecat has dark mask. Ferret has pale. Nocturnal. Road casualties. Don't expect to see one.
